title: Fn overloading: resolution logic
analysis: Function call resolution lives inline at lines 849-937 of reify-compiler/src/lib.rs within compile_expr_guarded(). Current flow: (1) compile args, (2) filter functions by name + arity + type_compatible() which includes Int→Real widening, (3) 1 match → UserFunctionCall, >1 → ambiguity error, 0 → stdlib FunctionCall fallback. The task requires: (A) exact type matching during resolution (no Int→Real), (B) proper error with candidate listing when user functions exist but none match, (C) ambiguity error when multiple match, (D) stdlib fallback only when NO user functions have that name. The type_compatible() function (lines 544-552) is only used in one place (the resolution filter), so replacing it with exact matching is safe. The evaluator in reify-expr (line 280) currently looks up functions by name+arity only, which is insufficient when two overloads have same name+arity but different param types. The evaluator already receives CompiledExpr args which carry result_type, so we can match on arg result_types to disambiguate — no struct changes to UserFunctionCall needed. Key insight: with exact matching, the current code would produce ambiguity for f(int_val) when both fn f(Int) and fn f(Real) exist, because type_compatible matches BOTH. Exact matching resolves this by only matching f(Int). The duplicate signature check (lines 1851-1875) is complementary and catches same-name + same-param-types at post-compilation.

prerequisites.description: Verify all existing compiler and expr tests pass on the worktree base before making changes. Run cargo test -p reify-compiler -p reify-expr to establish baseline.

steps.description: Write failing test in fn_overload_tests.rs: define fn f(x: Int)->Int and fn f(x: Real)->Real, call f(3) where 3 is Int. Assert resolves to UserFunctionCall with function_name 'f' and return type Int (not ambiguity error). This fails because current type_compatible matches BOTH overloads via Int→Real widening, producing an ambiguity error.

Extract resolve_function_overload() helper function from the inline FunctionCall resolution logic (lines 849-937). The function takes (name, arg_types, functions) and returns an enum: Resolved(&CompiledFunction) | NoUserFunctions | NoMatch(Vec<&CompiledFunction>) | Ambiguous(Vec<&CompiledFunction>). Use exact type matching (param_ty == arg_ty, NOT type_compatible) for candidate filtering. Wire the enum variants into the existing match arms: Resolved → UserFunctionCall, NoUserFunctions → stdlib FunctionCall fallback, NoMatch → error diagnostic, Ambiguous → ambiguity error.

Write failing test: define only fn f(x: Real)->Real, call f(3) where 3 is Int. Assert produces a diagnostic error containing 'no matching overload' and the candidate 'f(Real) -> Real'. This verifies Int→Real is NOT used during resolution and that zero-match errors list candidates. Fails because error message format may not include candidates yet.

Ensure the NoMatch error diagnostic in resolve_function_overload lists all same-name candidates with their full signatures: 'no matching overload for f(Int), candidates: f(Real) -> Real'. Format each candidate as 'name(param_types) -> return_type'.

Write test: define fn f(x: Int)->Int and fn f(x: Int, y: Int)->Int, call f(5) with one arg. Assert resolves to the 1-arity overload (UserFunctionCall with return type Int). This verifies arity-based disambiguation. Should pass from step-2 implementation.
Verify step-5 test passes. If not, fix arity filtering in resolve_function_overload. Also write an additional test: call f(1, 2) → selects the 2-arity overload.

Write test: no user function named 'sqrt', call sqrt(4.0) in a structure let binding. Assert compiles to FunctionCall (stdlib) with qualified_name 'std::sqrt', NOT an error. This verifies stdlib fallback is preserved when no user functions have the name. Should pass from step-2 NoUserFunctions path.

Verify step-7 test passes. If it fails, ensure resolve_function_overload returns NoUserFunctions (not NoMatch) when zero user functions have the given name, and that the caller falls through to stdlib FunctionCall as before.

Write failing test: define fn f(x: Int)->Int and fn f(x: Int)->Real (same name and param types, different return types — i.e., duplicate signatures). Call f(3). Assert produces an ambiguity error diagnostic that lists both candidate signatures. This tests the ambiguity error path with candidate listing.

Ensure the Ambiguous error diagnostic lists all matching candidate signatures: 'ambiguous function call: 2 candidates match f(Int): f(Int) -> Int, f(Int) -> Real'. Format each candidate with return type for clarity.

Write failing E2E test: define fn double(x: Int)->Int { x * 2 } and fn double(x: Real)->Real { x * 2.0 }, call both as structure let bindings: let a = double(3) and let b = double(3.0). Parse, compile (assert no errors), then evaluate with reify-expr and verify a==Int(6) and b==Real(6.0). This tests full pipeline including evaluator disambiguation. Fails because evaluator matches by name+arity only, picking the first 'double' for both calls.

Update eval_user_function_call() in reify-expr/src/lib.rs (line 280) to match on param types in addition to name and arity: change find closure from 'f.name == function_name && f.params.len() == args.len()' to also check 'f.params.iter().zip(args.iter()).all(|((_, pt), arg)| *pt == arg.result_type)'. This ensures the evaluator selects the same overload the compiler resolved.

design_decisions.decision: Extract resolve_function_overload() as a named helper returning a 4-variant enum
design_decisions.rationale: The task says 'extend resolve_function()' but resolution is currently inline. Extracting a named function with Resolved/NoUserFunctions/NoMatch/Ambiguous variants improves readability, testability (unit-testable with constructed inputs), and makes the control flow explicit. Each variant maps cleanly to one compiled output or diagnostic.

design_decisions.decision: No struct changes to UserFunctionCall — evaluator uses compiled arg result_types for disambiguation
design_decisions.rationale: UserFunctionCall { function_name, args } is referenced in ~20 pattern-match sites across 5 crates (reify-types, reify-compiler, reify-expr, reify-eval, gui). Adding param_types would require touching all of them. Instead, since the compiler uses exact type matching, the compiled args' result_types exactly equal the selected overload's param types. The evaluator can match on these existing result_types with zero struct changes.

design_decisions.decision: Exact type equality (==) for resolution, not type_compatible()
design_decisions.rationale: The task explicitly states 'Int→Real promotion is NOT considered during overload resolution.' The existing type_compatible() allows Int→Real widening. Using == for resolution and preserving type_compatible() unchanged avoids breaking any other callers (though currently there are none). This makes overload selection unambiguous: f(Int) and f(Real) are distinct overloads.

design_decisions.decision: Stdlib fallback only when zero user functions share the name
design_decisions.rationale: The task says 'zero matches → error with candidates listed.' This applies only when user-defined overloads EXIST but none match. When no user functions have the name at all (e.g., sin, cos, sqrt), the existing stdlib FunctionCall fallback is preserved. This avoids breaking all stdlib function calls.

design_decisions.decision: Error messages include full candidate signatures with return types
design_decisions.rationale: Listing candidates as 'f(Int, Int) -> Int' rather than just 'f(Int, Int)' helps the user understand the available overloads. Return types are included because two overloads might have the same param types but different returns (caught as duplicate signature, but the error should still be informative).

reuse.how: The stdlib fallback path (ResolvedFunction, qualified_name, is_geometry_function check, content hash computation) is preserved verbatim for the NoUserFunctions case. Only the user-function resolution path changes.
The existing ambiguity error format is the template for both NoMatch and Ambiguous error diagnostics. Extend with candidate signature listing using the same format!() + join pattern.

The Resolved case emits UserFunctionCall identically to the current single-match case: same content hash computation, same struct fields. Only the caller (resolve_function_overload) changes, not the emission.

Each compiled arg carries result_type which, under exact resolution, equals the selected overload's param type. The evaluator's find() closure adds a zip-check of param types against arg result_types for O(1) disambiguation.

Follow the established pattern: parse source string, compile, assert no errors, find template, find value cell, pattern-match on CompiledExprKind::UserFunctionCall { function_name, args }. Error tests filter diagnostics by Severity::Error and assert on message content.
Preserved unchanged. It catches same-name + same-param-type duplicates post-compilation, complementing the runtime ambiguity detection during resolution. Both error paths are informative.
